summary: Runtime/docs drift remains: AGENTS and parts of docs still mention removed monolith files, stale scheduler descriptions, and prompt artifact assumptions that no longer match the current modular runtime.

Operational maturity is still fragile: deploy remains manual by documentation, external feeds stay volatile, and no executable automated test suite exists for connectors, gates, or cron route behavior.

Current visual/runtime confirmation is partial: public pages and headers were verified over local HTTP, but fresh browser screenshots and formal accessibility/performance evidence were not collected in this session.

Current screenshot baseline is stale and partially contradicts the live runtime truth: observed image sets still show the old three-slot cadence and older screen compositions, so visual QA lacks a trustworthy current-state capture set.

Homepage hierarchy over-invests in manifesto and schedule framing before proving value with the live feed. The content feed is pushed below hero and rhythm surfaces, weakening first-view clarity for a product whose core proof is fresh posts.

Interactive elements lacked clear focus-visible styling. The audit added explicit focus outlines and glow treatment for links and buttons, but a fresh screenshot-based visual recheck is still missing.

Archive was silently truncated to the same latest-24 slice used by the homepage feed. The audit split archive fetching from the capped home query so `/archive` now reads the full post set, but broader long-history runtime recheck is still limited to local smoke evidence.

Reader-facing discovery and retention surface remains thin: the public site has category filtering and archive browsing, but no public RSS/feed route, no search surface, and no built-in subscription entrypoint for following new posts.

Observability for performance and reliability is too thin: cron has console logs and trace_id fields, but there are no traces, metrics, latency histograms, or backlog counters to detect degraded publishing loops quickly.

Ingest tail latency remains fragile under degraded dependencies because topic source failover is sequential and retry behavior is uneven across sources. This is a performance and reliability risk for authorized cron runs even though public pages are locally fast.

Connector timeout helpers kept success-path timers alive until timeout expiry. The audit replaced raw timeout signals with cancellable timeout controllers and revalidated build plus public-route burst sanity, but the fix still lacks measurement on the real upstream-heavy cron path.

Release operations are still under-documented and under-instrumented: there is no explicit rollback procedure, no backup/restore proof, and no alerting/tracing baseline for the production publishing loop.

Release documentation drifts from current runtime truth. The old launch checklist still documents Vercel Hobby cron assumptions, while current scheduling truth lives in GitHub Actions and newer state docs.
The project lacked a formal health endpoint for release smoke checks. The audit added `/api/health` and wired it into `pre-launch-check.sh`, then revalidated build and local health probing.
